questions.text: Hazrat Umar (R.A) divided his state in how much administrative units/provinces.
questions.subject: Pakistan Studies

options.explanation: Hazrat Umar, the second Caliph of Islam, divided his state into several administrative units known as "provinces" or "governorates." Initially, he divided the Islamic empire into 8 provinces,

Hazrat Umar, the second Caliph of Islam, divided his state into several administrative units known as "provinces" or "governorates." Initially, he divided the Islamic empire into 8 provinces,

Hazrat Umar, the second Caliph of Islam, divided his state into several administrative units known as "provinces" or "governorates." Initially, he divided the Islamic empire into 8 provinces, each governed by a governor appointed by him. This administrative division helped in the efficient governance of the expanding Islamic empire and facilitated the implementation of Islamic principles and justice.

questions.text: According to the constitution of 1973, age for president of Pakistan must not be less than.....years.

questions.text: There were princely states in India at the time of partition of India.
questions.subject: Pakistan Studies

options.explanation: At the time of partition in 1947, there were around 562 princely states in India.

At the time of partition in 1947, there were around 562 princely states in India. These princely states were semi-autonomous regions ruled by local monarchs or princes under the suzerainty of the British Crown. After India gained independence, these princely states were given the choice to join either India or Pakistan, or to remain independent. Eventually, through a process of integration, most of these states acceded to either India or Pakistan.
At the time of partition in 1947, there were around 562 princely states in India.

questions.text: On the call of Quaid-e-Azam,. Muslims of India observed day of deliverance on.
questions.subject: Pakistan Studies

options.explanation: On the call of Quaid-e-Azam Muhammad Ali Jinnah, Muslims of India observed the "Day of Deliverance" on December 22, 1939. This day was observed as a protest against the Congress ministries' resignations in protest against the British government's decision to involve India in World War II without consulting Indian leaders. Muslims viewed this day as a deliverance from what they perceived as Congress domination.
